title: How to Use Intuition When Making Business Decisions
meta_description: Trust your gut in business without ignoring the facts. Learn to blend intuition with logic for smarter decisions.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Business Decisions
og_description: Trust your gut in business without ignoring the facts. Learn to blend intuition with logic for smarter decisions.
body:
You Already Know More Than You Think

You're sitting in front of a spreadsheet that says one thing. Your gut is screaming another. And you're stuck. Right?! That moment of paralysis between what looks logical on paper and what feels true in your body - that's where most business owners live when they're trying to make a big decision.

I'm Kalesh, and I've been teaching people like you - intuitive, sensitive, deep feelers - how to trust what they already know for over a decade. And here's the thing nobody tells you about using intuition in business: it's not about abandoning logic. It's about adding another layer of information that your rational mind can't access on its own.

Your nervous system picks up on things your conscious brain filters out. The slight hesitation in a client's voice. The energy shift when you walk into a meeting. The way a partnership opportunity makes your chest tighten even though the numbers look perfect. That's not anxiety. That's data. Does that land?

So let's get into how you actually use this stuff when real money, real people, and real consequences are on the line.

Stop Treating Intuition Like a Magic 8-Ball

First thing we need to clear up: intuition isn't a shortcut to certainty. It's not going to hand you a winning lottery ticket or tell you exactly which stock to buy. If that's what you're looking for, you're going to be disappointed.

Intuition works more like a compass than a GPS. A compass tells you direction, not the exact route. It points you toward what's aligned with your deeper knowing, but you still have to walk the path yourself. You still have to do the research, run the numbers, talk to the people, and make the calls.

What intuition does do is flag the things your rational mind misses. It's the whisper that says "something's off here" when the deal looks too good to be true. It's the quiet "yes" that settles in your chest when you're considering a risk that actually fits you.

The mistake most people make is they try to use intuition to replace thinking. That's not how it works. You don't get to skip the hard work. You just get an extra set of eyes on the situation - eyes that can see around corners.

Why Business Logic Alone Fails You

Here's a hard truth: pure logic in business is a lie. Every major decision you make involves variables you can't quantify. Market timing. Human behavior. Cultural shifts. The hidden motivations of the people you're working with.

Spreadsheets can't account for the fact that your potential business partner has a history of cutting people out of deals - unless that history is documented somewhere. But your intuition might catch it in the way they avoid eye contact when talking about past partnerships. Or in the way your stomach drops when they say "trust me."

I've worked with dozens of entrepreneurs who ignored their gut on a hire, a partnership, or a product launch. Every single one of them came back later and said the same thing: "I knew. I knew before I signed, before I committed, before I spent the money. I just didn't trust myself."

That's the wound we're here to heal. The Bright Wound - that sensitivity you've been told is a weakness your whole life - is actually your most valuable business asset when you learn how to interpret it correctly.

The Ground Rules for Intuitive Business Decisions

Before we get into the how, let's set some ground rules. These are non-negotiable if you want to use intuition without losing your shirt.

Rule 1: Intuition Is Not an Emergency Response

Real intuition doesn't scream. It whispers. If you're feeling panic, urgency, or pressure to decide right now, that's not your intuition. That's your survival brain trying to protect you from something - and it's often wrong about what the actual threat is.

Salespeople know this. They create urgency to override your gut. "This offer expires today." "Someone else is about to take this deal." "You need to decide now." That's manipulation, not intuition. Real intuitive knowing is calm. It's steady. It doesn't need you to rush.

Rule 2: Your Body Knows Before Your Mind Does

Your brain processes information at about 60 bits per second. Your nervous system processes millions. So by the time you "think" something is wrong, your body has already registered it. That's why you get a headache before you realize you're in a bad meeting. Or why you feel tired around a certain client even though they're perfectly nice.

Start paying attention to your physical responses. Where in your body do you feel a "yes"? Where do you feel a "no"? For most people, a yes is an opening sensation in the chest or belly. A no is a tightening, a closing, a pulling away. Your signals might be different. Learn them.

Rule 3: You Have to Separate Fear from Intuition

This is the hardest part. Fear and intuition can feel almost identical in the body. Both can make your stomach drop. Both can make your heart race. Both can make you want to run.

The difference is in the quality of the sensation. Fear is scattered, frantic, and future-focused. It's "what if everything goes wrong?" Intuition is focused, clear, and present. It's "this isn't right" or "this is right" without the story attached.

One way to tell them apart: ask yourself "What am I actually afraid of?" If you can name a specific consequence, it's probably fear. If the knowing just sits there without a story, it's intuition.

How to Actually Use Intuition in Your Business Decisions

Alright, let's get practical. Here's a step-by-step process you can use the next time you're facing a big decision.

Step 1: Gather All the Rational Information First

This might surprise you, but I never recommend making an intuitive decision without doing the homework first. You need to know the numbers. You need to understand the risks. You need to talk to the people involved. Your intuition needs something to work with - it's not a magic wand.

So start with the spreadsheet. Read the contract. Do the market research. Get clear on what the logical choice looks like. Then set it aside.

Step 2: Get Quiet and Check Your Body

Once you have all the information, give yourself space to feel into it. This can be five minutes or five days, depending on the decision. Sit with the choice. Imagine saying "yes" and notice what happens in your body. Then imagine saying "no" and notice what happens.

Don't judge what comes up. Just observe. Is there a relaxation? A tightening? A sense of expansion or contraction? Your body is giving you feedback. Listen to it.

If you struggle with this, try journaling. Write down everything you're feeling without editing. Sometimes the truth comes out when you stop trying to control the narrative.

Step 3: Look for Patterns, Not Single Hits

One intuitive hit doesn't mean much. A pattern of hits over time means everything. Start keeping a simple log of your intuitive impressions and what happened next. Over weeks and months, you'll start to see where your intuition is reliable and where it gets confused with fear or wishful thinking.

For example, you might notice that your gut is always right about people but wrong about timing. Or that you're good at sensing market trends but bad at reading contracts. Know your strengths and weaknesses.

I recommend picking up a copy of Caroline Myss's "Anatomy of the Spirit" for a deeper look at how your body's energy centers relate to decision-making. It's a classic for a reason. See on Amazon (paid link)

Step 4: Test Small Before You Go Big

If you're new to using intuition in business, don't start with a million-dollar decision. Start with small bets. Which vendor to use for a small project. Which time of day to schedule your important calls. Which email subject line to try.

Build your trust muscle. Every time you follow an intuitive hit and it works out, you're strengthening the connection. Every time you ignore it and regret it, you're learning too. But you want to learn on small stakes before you risk big ones.

Step 5: Use the "Sleep On It" Rule

For any significant decision, give yourself at least one overnight cycle. Your conscious mind will try to talk you into or out of things. Your subconscious - where intuition lives - needs time to process. Sleep resets your nervous system and often brings clarity.

I've lost count of how many clients have told me "I woke up and just knew." That's not magic. That's your deeper intelligence having room to surface. Create that room.

"Intuition doesn't tell you what to do. It tells you what's true. Your job is to have the courage to act on that truth, even when it doesn't make sense to anyone else."

When Your Intuition Contradicts the Data

This is where it gets real. You've done the research. The numbers look good. Everyone says go. But something in you says no. What do you do?

First, don't ignore the data. But don't ignore the feeling either. The most successful intuitive business people I know treat this as a signal to dig deeper, not as a reason to stop.

Ask yourself: "What am I sensing that the data might be missing?" Maybe the market research is from last year and things have shifted. Maybe the person presenting the opportunity has a hidden agenda. Maybe you're picking up on something that hasn't happened yet but is about to.

Sometimes the answer is to delay. Sometimes it's to change the terms. Sometimes it's to walk away entirely. But you won't know until you investigate why the contradiction exists.

I've seen people save their entire businesses by listening to that contradiction. And I've seen people lose everything by ignoring it. The choice is yours.

Building Your Intuition Muscles Over Time

Intuition is like any other skill - you get better with practice. But most people never practice because they don't know how. Here are a few exercises that actually work.

Exercise 1: The Daily Check-In

Every morning, before you check your email or look at your calendar, sit for two minutes and ask yourself: "What do I need to know today?" Don't force an answer. Just ask the question and notice what comes up. It might be a word, a feeling, an image, or nothing at all. The practice is the point.

Exercise 2: The Yes/No Test

For one week, before every small decision - what to eat, which route to drive, which task to do first - check in with your body. Say the options out loud and notice your physical response. You'll start to see patterns in how your body communicates yes and no.

Exercise 3: The Intuition Journal

Keep a running log of your intuitive hits. Write down what you sensed, when you sensed it, and what actually happened. Over time, you'll build a track record that proves your intuition is real. This is especially helpful when doubt creeps in.

For a structured approach to developing your intuitive skills, I recommend Sonia Choquette's "Trust Your Vibes". She breaks down the process in a way that's practical and grounded. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Hardest Part: Acting on What You Know

Let's be honest. Most of the time, you already know what the right decision is. The problem isn't knowing. The problem is having the courage to act on that knowing when the people around you are telling you something different.

That's the Bright Wound in action. You're sensitive enough to pick up on truth that others miss. But that sensitivity also makes you doubt yourself. You've been trained your whole life to override your own knowing in favor of what's "reasonable" or "logical" or "what everyone else is doing."

Using intuition in business isn't just about making better decisions. It's about reclaiming your authority. It's about trusting that the same intelligence that grew a tree from a seed can guide you through a difficult negotiation.

Does that land?

I know it's scary. I know the stakes are high. But I also know that every time you choose your own knowing over someone else's certainty, you get a little stronger. And every time you do, you prove to yourself that you can be trusted.

That's the real work.

When You Still Can't Decide

Sometimes you do all the work and you still don't have clarity. That's okay. It doesn't mean you're broken or that intuition doesn't work. It means the timing isn't right, or you don't have enough information, or there's something you're not ready to see yet.

In those cases, the intuitive choice is often to wait. To gather more data. To let the situation develop. To trust that clarity will come when it's supposed to.

But here's the thing: waiting is also a decision. And it's one you can make with integrity. You don't have to force an answer before it's ready.

One tool that helps in these moments is Eckhart Tolle's "The Power of Now". It's not a business book, but it teaches you how to get out of your head and into the present moment - which is exactly where intuition lives. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Bottom Line

Using intuition in business isn't about being psychic. It's about being present. It's about listening to the information your body and your deeper awareness are giving you, and having the courage to act on it.

You don't have to choose between logic and intuition. You get to use both. The rational mind handles the known variables. Intuition handles the unknown ones. Together, they make you a better decision-maker than either one alone.

Start small. Practice daily. Trust the patterns. And most of all, stop apologizing for being sensitive. That sensitivity is not a weakness. It's the thing that lets you see what others can't. Use it.

If you want to go deeper into this work - to understand your specific intuitive style and how to apply it to your business and life - I've put together a short assessment that maps out your natural strengths. It takes about five minutes, and it'll show you exactly where you're already intuitive and where you need to build trust. Take the assessment here.
